Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic but intense. I'm 28, working and doing nine online courses, so I had to carve out these marathon study sessions whenever I could—usually late nights or early mornings before my other responsibilities kicked in. I'd dedicate around 32 hours a week, but it wasn't spread evenly at all. I'd listen to lectures on repeat, sometimes while commuting or doing chores, because that's how I absorb information best. The routine was less of a schedule and more of a survival strategy.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening in my life at any given moment. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then I'd go into overdrive when deadlines loomed. Being a crisis learner means I've learned to function under pressure, but it's not sustainable and it terrifies me every single time. Some weeks I'd study 50 hours, others maybe 10—it completely depended on external demands.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in, if I'm being completely honest. I'd get an email reminder about a deadline or realize I hadn't checked announcements in days, and that scared feeling would drive me to the LMS. Sometimes it was the gamified tasks that drew me in—those little achievement badges gave me a dopamine hit when everything else felt overwhelming. Fear of missing something important was probably my biggest motivator.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a comfortable routine for me—it always felt like entering a space where I was behind on something. The closest I got to routine was checking it every few days when the gamified elements were introduced, because earning points felt like tangible progress even when I was drowning. But honestly, logging in usually spiked my anxiety rather than feeling like a normal habit.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could download them and listen multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also helpful when I actually engaged—reading how other students understood the material gave me different perspectives. The gamified tasks surprisingly helped too; they broke down overwhelming content into smaller, achievable chunks that felt less scary.
Q6: I consistently ignored the reading materials in their original PDF form—I just couldn't focus on dense text when I was already anxious. I'd often skip the supplementary resources because I was barely keeping up with required content. The peer interaction features went largely unused by me because I felt embarrassed about how behind I was and didn't want others to see that. I only had three peer interactions the entire course.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to assessments or when it gave me that sense of accomplishment through the gamification system. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd be tested on got pushed aside because I simply didn't have the bandwidth for anything that wasn't directly helping me pass.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're scared and overwhelmed and juggling nine courses, optional means 'skip this.' Required activities carried weight because they affected my grade, so they got prioritized even when I was exhausted. The gamified optional tasks were the exception—they felt like little rewards rather than additional burdens, so I actually engaged with some of those.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it constantly—on walks, while cooking, before bed. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement concepts I was struggling with. My LMS activity probably shows maybe 30% of my actual study time.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was intensely studying but barely logged into the LMS. I'd have downloaded everything I needed and was working through it offline, listening to recordings on repeat, making audio notes to myself. My 83 minutes of video watch time doesn't reflect the hours I spent with those same videos downloaded to my phone. The system couldn't see me pacing my apartment, reciting concepts out loud to memorize them.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture once to download it, then listen to it maybe ten more times offline over the following days. Same with any PDFs—I'd print them or save them locally and work from those copies. My single login to access something translated to hours of engagement that happened completely outside the LMS's view. It was more efficient for me than repeatedly streaming content.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. Around midterms, my anxiety peaked and I could barely bring myself to log in because seeing how much I had to do paralyzed me. When multiple assignment deadlines clustered together across my nine courses, I'd shut down emotionally. My attendance dropped to under 40% during those times—not because I wasn't trying, but because I was scared and overwhelmed.
Q13: Massively. The cognitive load of nine courses meant I was constantly triaging what needed attention most urgently. When I was exhausted, the LMS felt like this overwhelming maze I couldn't navigate efficiently. Competing responsibilities—work, family obligations—meant I'd sometimes go days without logging in, then binge-access everything in a panic. The confusion of juggling multiple course structures definitely affected how consistently I engaged.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd log in, click through materials, but my brain was elsewhere—worrying about other deadlines, too anxious to actually absorb anything. Sometimes I'd watch videos with my eyes glazed over, technically present but not learning. Those sessions felt like going through motions to feel less guilty rather than genuine engagement. The fear drove me to the platform but didn't always translate to actual learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. The idea of putting my thoughts out there when I felt so behind was terrifying—what if everyone could tell I was struggling? I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last possible moment because beginning them made the scope of work feel real and overwhelming. Avoidance was my anxiety's defense mechanism, even when it made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire operating system. I'd be relatively dormant on the LMS until a deadline approached, then my activity would spike dramatically. The panic of an approaching due date was often the only thing that could override my anxiety about engaging. I'd submit assignments with hours to spare, having done most of the work in an intense burst. It wasn't healthy, but it got me to a Distinction somehow.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback on my exam scores—I got over 90%—gave me brief moments of relief and validation that I wasn't failing. But critical feedback sometimes sent me into spirals where I'd avoid the course for days. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal—just points and badges rather than judgment of my capabilities as a student.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having clear modules helped me know what to prioritize, but the sheer volume across nine courses meant I couldn't engage with everything as designed. The gamified elements actually helped me engage when I otherwise might have avoided the platform entirely. Structured deadlines forced me to show up even when every instinct said to hide.
Q19: The gamification made engagement significantly easier—it gave me small wins when everything else felt like drowning. However, the volume of required readings and the expectation of regular attendance was harder for someone like me who was managing so much. Discussion requirements were particularly challenging because they demanded consistent presence I couldn't maintain. Flexible deadline policies, where they existed, were lifesavers.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, clicking through things to feel like they're doing something, but too overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. High activity can be a sign of struggling, not thriving. They might be searching for something that helps, trying different resources, but unable to translate that exploration into finished assignments. Fear drives a lot of clicking.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS engagement was technically 'low' but I got a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials once and study them extensively elsewhere. My exam score was over 90% despite minimal visible platform activity because I was learning—just not in ways the system could track. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accesses materials and submits work—the mechanical interactions. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't see that I listened to a lecture fifteen times or that I logged in paralyzed with anxiety. They show attendance but not attention, access but not absorption.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just clicking through it. For me, it's those moments when I'm actively listening, questioning, connecting concepts—usually happening with headphones in, away from my computer. True engagement is when learning feels meaningful rather than just performative. It's the difference between surviving a course and actually growing from it.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was throughout this entire experience, or how much courage it took just to keep going. It can't capture the hours of audio learning, the notebooks filled with handwritten notes, the late nights talking myself through panic attacks before logging in. It doesn't show that my low attendance and engagement scores coexisted with genuine, deep learning that happened in ways the platform couldn't see. My Distinction came from effort that was largely invisible to the system.
